title: Tarka dhal
subtitle: Red lentils simmered with turmeric, finished with a spiced butter tarka
excerpt: Tarka dhal is red lentils cooked to a thick, golden porridge with turmeric and cumin, finished with a tarka — a sizzle of butter, garlic, and whole spices poured over the top at the last moment. The tarka is what lifts it from simple lentil soup to something with depth and crackle.
sourceNotes:
• Madhur Jaffrey, *Indian Cookery* (1982). Primary reference for the tarka technique (the method of pouring hot, spice-infused fat over cooked lentils) and the balance of whole spices in the finishing stage.
• The dhal itself draws on the north Indian tradition of masoor dhal (red lentil dhal), which is one of the most common everyday dishes in the subcontinent. The tarka finishing step is universal across dhal preparations, though the spices and fat used vary by region.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight and reheat gently with a splash of water, as the lentils thicken on standing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles without adjustment. Make the tarka in a larger quantity and pour over each bowl separately.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dhal keeps in the fridge for 4 days. Make the tarka fresh each time — it only takes 2 minutes.

glossaryTerms.definition: Also written 'tadka' or 'chaunk'. A finishing technique in Indian cooking where whole spices, garlic, or dried chilli are briefly fried in hot fat until they crackle and release their volatile oils, then poured over the finished dish. The sizzling oil acts as a flavour carrier and adds textural contrast.

Split legumes (lentils, peas, or beans) and the dishes made from them. Masoor dhal (split red lentils) is the most common variety for quick cooking — it dissolves into a thick porridge in 20 minutes without soaking.

content.text: is one of the most forgiving things to cook: red lentils need no soaking, no real technique, and thirty minutes. The lentils simmer with turmeric and cumin until they dissolve into a golden, silky base. Everything else happens in the last two minutes.

is a small pan of hot butter with cumin seeds, dried chilli, and garlic — fried for 30 seconds until the seeds crackle and the garlic colours, then poured over the surface of the dhal. The hiss when it hits the lentils is the point. Do not skip it.
Serve with basmati rice, naan, or chapati. The dhal should be thick enough to mound slightly in the bowl but still flow when a spoon is dragged through it.

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a medium saucepan over a medium heat. Cook {{onion}} diced onion for 8 minutes until golden. Add {{garlic}} of garlic (for the dhal) and {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger. Cook for 2 minutes. Add {{turmeric}} and {{cumin-ground}}. Stir for 1 minute.

Add {{red-lentils}} of rinsed lentils and {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tomatoes. Pour in 750 ml of water and bring to a boil. Reduce to a simmer and cook uncovered for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring every few minutes as the lentils begin to dissolve. Add more water if the dhal catches on the base before the lentils have fully broken down. Season generously with salt.

When the dhal is ready, make the tarka immediately before serving. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a small frying pan over a high heat until it foams. Add {{cumin-seeds}}, {{dried-chilli}}, and {{garlic}} of sliced garlic (for the tarka). Fry for 30 to 45 seconds, stirring once, until the seeds crackle and the garlic turns gold at the edges. Pour directly over the surface of the dhal — it will sizzle and spatter. Add coriander if using. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Dhal is glue-thick and impossible to stir
items.fix: Add boiling water a ladle at a time, stirring to loosen. Dhal thickens significantly as it cools — what looks right in the pan will be thicker in the bowl. Err on the side of looser.

items.symptom: Tarka garlic has burned and turned bitter
items.cause: Heat was too high or the garlic was left unattended for too long.
items.fix: The tarka takes no more than 45 seconds. Have everything measured and ready before the butter goes in. If it burns, discard and start again — it takes one minute, and burned tarka ruins the dhal.

items.symptom: Lentils have not dissolved after 25 minutes
items.cause: Heat was too low, or the water was not kept at a steady simmer.
items.fix: Red lentils dissolve quickly at a real simmer — not a gentle bubble, but a proper rolling simmer. If they are still whole after 25 minutes, increase the heat and add a splash more water to prevent sticking.

content.text: Dhal in some form is eaten across the Indian subcontinent: lentils, split peas, or dried beans cooked with spice and tempered with hot fat. Masoor dhal with a butter tarka is northern Indian in origin, and the tarka technique specifically is one of the oldest and most widely used in Indian cooking. The version most familiar in Britain comes through the British-Indian restaurant tradition, where tarka dhal appears as a side dish alongside meat curries. At home it is as often a main course in itself — cheaper, faster, and more satisfying than anything it typically accompanies.
